concept: Relapse / Return or Worsening After Improvement in the Same Course
description: A return or worsening after improvement, within the same understood course. The word names a road doubling back; it carries no blame, and it does not erase whatever ground was gained before the return.
sound_symbolism: Body-linked 'mo' moves through reopening 'ra' into sharp 'shi', giving the course a felt return without sounding like blame. The form turns back but does not close the future.
grammatical_notes: An intransitive verb: 'shia to morashi.' (They relapsed.) The event-noun rule supplies a relapse. State the returning condition, prior improvement, timing, evidence, and uncertainty where relevant. Distinct from 'helanu' (recover), 'turema' (return), 'nurawi' (chronic), an ordinary setback, and a new diagnosis.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A changing course can turn without making prior effort meaningless or defining the person by recurrence.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Removing blame from relapse supports honest reporting, continued care, and revision rather than punishment or shame.

tags.physical: return or worsening of a health condition after improvement
tags.temporal: a reversal within an ongoing health course
